Market Impact & Price Trajectory: Triggered the First Gulf War Oil Shock. Crude prices doubled from roughly $15 to a spike of over $40/bbl on physical supply loss fears.
A collapse in Asian manufacturing and currency valuations crippled global crude demand, sending prices into a multi-year downturn toward $10/bbl.
Prompted an immediate near-term collapse in global aviation demand and travel, driving immediate structural downward pressure on refined product spreads.
The outbreak of the Second Gulf War severely disrupted Iraqi export capacity and introduced a long-term risk premium to Middle Eastern supply lines.
Direct military attacks on Chevron and Shell facilities in Nigeria knocked out nearly 25% of the country’s daily oil production, shocking sweet crude markets.
Driven by aggressive emerging market demand (China/India) coupled with speculative capital inflows, driving Brent to a record high of $147.50/bbl by July 2008.
Marked the peak of the global financial crisis. Global oil demand collapsed virtually overnight, forcing prices down from peak levels to $36/bbl by December.
Sparked widespread Middle East instability and completely halted Libyan sweet crude exports, driving Brent back up over the $100/bbl threshold.
The EU and US imposed a strict embargo on Iranian oil imports, restricting global supply balances and forcing Asian buyers to re-route supply chains.
US tight oil production reached multi-decade highs, flipping global markets into a severe oversupply. This initiated a massive 70% price structural breakdown.
Facing sustained low prices, OPEC joined forces with non-OPEC producers (led by Russia) to coordinate deep production cuts, establishing a artificial price floor.
Drone strikes targeted major Saudi Aramco processing plants, temporarily taking out 5.7 million barrels per day—the largest single sudden supply disruption in history.
Alliance talks collapsed as Russia rejected output cuts. Saudi Arabia responded by flooding the market at discounted rates, causing a historical pricing rout.
Global transportation came to a complete halt, creating a unprecedented demand destruction event. This triggered the historic sub-zero storage settlement for WTI futures.
Triggered comprehensive Western sanctions on Russian energy exports. This forced a massive restructuring of global flows and pushed Brent past $130/bbl.
